speaker: SAM
text: Right now, while you're sitting there — about 37 trillion things are keeping you alive.

speaker: MAYA
text: 37 trillion.

speaker: SAM
text: Give or take. And every single one of them has a job.

speaker: MAYA
text: Those are your cells. And they've been running since before you were born.

speaker: SAM
text: Before you could walk. Before you could talk. Before you existed as anything you'd recognize as "you."

speaker: MAYA
text: 37 trillion tiny things, working, all at once, right now — and you weren't even thinking about it until I just said it.

speaker: SAM
text: You're welcome.

speaker: MAYA
text: Let's get into it.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: MAYA
text: Scientists didn't always know living things were made of cells. For most of human history, nobody knew.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: SAM
text: What changed?

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: MAYA
text: A microscope. Robert Hooke, 1665. He looked at a slice of cork and saw tiny rooms.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: SAM
text: He called them "cells" because they looked like monks' rooms in a monastery.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: MAYA
text: So the word "cell" is basically medieval architecture.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: SAM
text: Yeah... essentially.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: MAYA
text: And from there, scientists eventually agreed on three things. One — all living things are made of cells.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: SAM
text: Two — the cell is the smallest unit of life. Below that, you're just talking chemistry.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: MAYA
text: And three — all cells come from pre-existing cells.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: SAM
text: Nothing alive comes from nothing.

beat: BEAT 2 — WHAT IS A CELL, REALLY
speaker: MAYA
text: Yeah... essentially.

speaker: MAYA
text: Okay, so — picture a cell. Most people picture a blob. Forget the blob completely.

speaker: SAM
text: Working on it.

speaker: MAYA
text: It's more like a city. Everything's got a job. Everything depends on everything else.

speaker: SAM
text: Alright. Give me the tour.

speaker: MAYA
text: Start at the top. The nucleus — that's city hall. It holds the D-N-A, which is basically the master plan for the whole operation. Nothing happens without city hall signing off.

speaker: SAM
text: Very bureaucratic.

speaker: MAYA
text: It kind of is, actually.

speaker: SAM
text: Is there a border? Cities have borders.

speaker: MAYA
text: There is — the cell membrane. It's the checkpoint. Decides what gets in — water, nutrients, oxygen — and what gets kicked out.

speaker: SAM
text: Not everybody gets through.

speaker: MAYA
text: That's the whole point of having one.

speaker: SAM
text: What powers the city?

speaker: MAYA
text: Mitochondria. They take glucose — fuel — and turn it into something the cell can actually run on, called A-T-P.

speaker: SAM
text: So we don't run on food directly.

speaker: MAYA
text: Not directly. We run on what the mitochondria make from it.

speaker: SAM
text: Huh. That actually explains something. When you're sick and just wiped out, even lying still —

speaker: MAYA
text: Your mitochondria are working overtime. We'll get into that one later.

speaker: SAM
text: Noted.

speaker: MAYA
text: Then there's the cytoplasm — just the streets. Fills the space, keeps everything suspended and connected. Not glamorous —

speaker: SAM
text: But necessary?

speaker: MAYA
text: Exactly! And the last stop — the vacuole. Storage. Animal cells have small ones scattered around. Plant cells have one enormous one, right in the middle.

speaker: SAM
text: And why does that matter?

speaker: MAYA
text: Because in a plant, that one vacuole takes up most of the space inside the cell. It's basically a water tank — when it's full, it pushes outward and keeps the whole thing rigid.

speaker: SAM
text: So that's why a plant droops when it's thirsty.

speaker: MAYA
text: That's exactly why.

speaker: SAM
text: Here's the thing. Plant cells and animal cells have a lot in common. But plants solved a problem animals never had.

speaker: MAYA
text: What problem?

speaker: SAM
text: They can't move. Can't chase food. They have to make it.

speaker: MAYA
text: First, the cell wall. Outside the membrane. Rigid. Made of cellulose.

speaker: SAM
text: That's why wood is wood.

speaker: MAYA
text: Basically, yeah! In an animal cell, the membrane is the outer edge, and it's flexible. In a plant cell, there's a wall outside that — hard, structured.

speaker: SAM
text: And the solar panels?

speaker: MAYA
text: Chloroplasts. They capture sunlight and turn it into glucose — photosynthesis. Every green thing you've ever seen is green because of chloroplasts.

speaker: SAM
text: Wait — if a plant's making its own food, why does it still need mitochondria?

speaker: MAYA
text: Because the chloroplast makes the glucose. The mitochondria still has to convert that glucose into A-T-P. Different jobs, same cell.

speaker: SAM
text: Division of labor.

speaker: MAYA
text: Very civic of them.

speaker: SAM
text: And the water tank we already covered.

speaker: MAYA
text: The vacuole, yes. Plants get one enormous vacuole.

speaker: MAYA
text: So the animal cell's more flexible — literally. The plant cell's more self-sufficient. Neither one's better. They just solved different problems.

speaker: SAM
text: Fair trade either way.

speaker: MAYA
text: You've had a cold. You know that feeling where you're just lying there, not doing anything, and you're still exhausted?

speaker: SAM
text: Your cells are in a war.

speaker: MAYA
text: Kind of, yeah. Immune cells are multiplying, attacking. Mitochondria in every cell are running at capacity.

speaker: SAM
text: So your body's not resting. It's mobilizing.

speaker: MAYA
text: Right. Your immune cells are working overtime, and that work costs energy — the same A-T-P your muscles would normally use to keep you moving. Sick or not, your body's still spending the same fuel. It's just spending it somewhere else.

speaker: SAM
text: The same cells that let you run a kilometer are the ones keeping you alive when you can't get off the couch. They don't have an off switch.

speaker: MAYA
text: 37 trillion things. They were already running before this episode started.

speaker: SAM
text: And they'll keep running after it ends.

speaker: SAM
text: So. Cell theory. Three ideas. Took scientists 200 years to agree on them.

speaker: MAYA
text: All living things are made of cells — every living thing you've ever seen. Plant, animal, person.

speaker: MAYA
text: The cell is the smallest unit of life. Below this, it's chemistry. At this level, it's life.

speaker: MAYA
text: And all cells come from pre-existing cells. You started as one cell. One. That divided, and divided, and kept dividing until there were 37 trillion of them. All running. Right now.

speaker: SAM
text: Not bad for something you can't see.

speaker: MAYA
text: That is the cell.

speaker: SAM
text: See you next time.
